narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on their academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 35 (slightly under the 36.70 average), middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles of 1— and predicted an 82.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school—far below the average decile of about 5.5—suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance as coursework intensified. Next in importance was your Year 1 grade decile, also at the bottom 10% (versus the typical mid-range around 5.7), which signaled early academic struggles that the model sees as a red flag for bar success. Your LSAT score provided the strongest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward passing since it was close to the dataset average, showing solid aptitude in that area compared to many peers. Overall, your grades in school deciles stood out as much weaker than typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher, while your other factors like family income and full-time status aligned more closely with the norm. This combination made your profile riskier in the model's eyes than most applicants. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or enrolling in a post-baccalaureate program to rebuild your academic record with higher decile rankings. Targeted bar prep courses or community college classes could also demonstrate improvement. Many students in similar situations, including Black males from middle-income backgrounds, have succeeded by addressing early grade weaknesses head-on. Reach out to admissions counselors for feedback, and explore bridge programs designed for underrepresented applicants aiming for law school. With focused effort on these gaps, you can strengthen your next application and show the potential the model partially recognized in your LSAT.
